name: Galileo

overview.value: Galileo is a global satellite navigation system operated by the European Union Agency for the Space Programme (EUSPA) and the European Space Agency (ESA). The constellation flies in medium Earth orbit at approximately 23,222 km altitude across three orbital planes inclined 56 degrees. Galileo began offering Early Operational Capability on 15 December 2016 and provides an Open Service free to all users, alongside a High Accuracy Service, a Public Regulated Service for government-authorized users, and a Search and Rescue Service.

operator.value: European Union Agency for the Space Programme (EUSPA) and European Space Agency (ESA)

orbit.value: MEO, ~23,222 km altitude, three orbital planes, 56 degree inclination

service_type.value: Open Service (free), High Accuracy Service (20 cm precision, free since January 2023), Public Regulated Service (encrypted, government-authorized), and Search and Rescue Service

notes: Added 2026-07-13 on Florian's instruction, for competitive context against commercial connectivity/IoT constellations. Government-owned system: orbits deliberately set to null (MEO ~23,200 km would break the MCC 3D LEO view) and no celestrak fields filled. All sources used were Wikipedia (unscored) or the operator's own gsc-europa.eu constellation status page (official/first-party, unscored); no aggregator or single-press sourcing was needed, so no field carries an snr block.

generations.name: GIOVE
generations.text: GIOVE testbed satellites flew from 2005 to 2008 as part of Galileo's development.

generations.name: IOV
generations.text: IOV (In-Orbit Validation) satellites flew from 2011 to 2012.

generations.name: FOC
generations.text: FOC (Full Operational Capability) satellites, Galileo's operational generation, span 2014 to 2027.

generations.name: Galileo Second Generation
generations.text: 12 second-generation Galileo (G2G) satellites are in development, with deployment expected to begin in 2027.

events.headline: GIOVE-A, Galileo's first test satellite, launches
First Galileo IOV satellite (GSAT0101, Thijs) launches
Galileo starts offering Early Operational Capability, its initial services
Galileo High Accuracy Service, 20 cm precision, becomes free to all users
Ariane 6 launches Galileo 33 and 34, the most recent Galileo satellites
